lesson.menu_desc: Reading an incident report + present perfect for results -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 8 -- Handling a Technical Issue
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patrícia Xavier Lima Iwashita | Aula 8 -- Handling a Technical Issue

reading.rtitle: Incident Report #4821 — Login Failure on the Atlas Dashboard
paras: On Monday morning, the Atlas team opened a support ticket. Several users could not log in to their dashboard, and the system showed the same error again and again. For about two hours, the issue stopped the sales team from working, so the customer marked it as urgent. The account manager has read the ticket and has escalated it to our technical team.

The engineers have worked on the problem all morning. First, they have reproduced the bug on a test account, so they could see exactly what the user saw. Then they have checked the server logs, and the logs have shown the real root cause: a recent update changed one security setting by mistake. While the team looked for a permanent fix, they have also shared a simple workaround, so most users could log in again.

By the afternoon, the team has resolved the issue. They have applied a small fix to the security setting and have tested it twice. The account manager has called the customer to confirm that everything works again. The login problem has not come back, and the team has added a note to the ticket so the same mistake does not happen next time.
reading.source: Adapted for class use from a customer support incident report

gist.prompt: What is the best one-line summary of this report?
choices: The team could not find the cause, and the customer has left for another provider.
After a login issue, the team has found the root cause and resolved it the same day, with a workaround in between.
The login problem is still open, and so far no one has worked on the ticket.

items: The login issue stopped the sales team from working for a while.
For about two hours, the issue stopped the sales team from working.
The engineers fixed the problem without reproducing the bug first.
First, they reproduced the bug on a test account to see exactly what the user saw.
The server logs showed that a recent update had changed a security setting.
The logs showed the root cause: an update changed one security setting by mistake.
The team gave users a workaround only after the final fix was ready.
They shared the workaround while they were still looking for a permanent fix.
In your own work, who do you escalate a technical issue to, and at what point do you escalate it?
The team shared a workaround before the final fix. When is a quick workaround a good idea, and when is it risky?
The report ends by adding a note to the ticket. Why is it important to write down the root cause for next time?

listenings.text: Hi Patrícia, this is David from the Atlas team. I am leaving a quick message before our call. We have a problem this morning. Several people on the sales team cannot log in to the dashboard, and they all see the same error. It started around nine, and it has stopped their work for almost two hours now, so I have marked the ticket as urgent. I have not changed anything on our side, so I think the issue is on the platform. Could you tell me if your engineers have reproduced the bug, and if there is a workaround we can use right now? My team is waiting, and I would like an update as soon as you have one. Thanks, and talk soon.

Hi Patrícia, it is Sofia from technical support, with the update you asked for. Good news first: we have resolved the login issue. Here is the short story. We have reproduced the bug on a test account, so we could see the exact error. Then we have checked the server logs, and they have shown the real root cause. A recent update changed one security setting by mistake, and that blocked the login. While we looked for a permanent fix, we have shared a quick workaround, so most users could log in again within the hour. This afternoon we have applied the fix and tested it twice. Everything is stable now. I have added a full note to the ticket, so please tell David the problem has not come back. Let us follow up tomorrow to confirm.
